Congestion  Fee Hits  Truckers
Continued from page B1

Miller said trucking companies will absorb some of the extra costs from the tolls, but they will have to pass on a portion of the fees to customers such as restaurants and bars that already operate on thin margins.

Freight  companies and their customers say the charges will pile on costs for cargo that can't reach its destination any other way.
[Ta not taking the subway from the Bronx to Midtown Manhattan," said Andrew Rigie, executive director of New York City Hospitality Alliance, which represents restaurants, bars and nightclubs.a
"That truck has to come into the zone and our suppliers have told us this is going to increase their cost of doing business," Rigie said.
About 120,000 trucks and commercial vehicles enter the city each day, according to the
city transportation department.

John Luciani, an executive at A. Duie Pyle, a West Chester,Pa.-based trucking company focused on the Northeast that consolidates shipments from multiple customers in a single trailer, says New York City is already the most difficult and expensive city it operates in.

Luciani said that because of traffic, A. Duie Pyle's drivers there average eight deliveries and pickups a day, compared with an average of 23 daily deliveries and pickups across the wider region. He said average toll costs per delivery are almost three times higher in the city than regionwide.

in NYC is significant," he said.
City and state transporta tion officials say truckers can reduce costs by delivering overnight. The congestion tol for heavy-duty trucks drops to $5.40 during off-peak hours.
Carriers say they already have plenty of incentives to deliver overnight.
Ea space is available. But they say many customers can't ac cept overnight deliveries be cause they don't have over night staff or a secure locatior for unattended deliveries.
"It's just another tax and an other cost of doing business ir the Big Apple," Luciani said.

The firm is now marketing the practice anew after the divisiveness of the 2024 election and a backlash against some policies embraced by progressives. The group helps employers "lower the temperature" around hot-button social and political issues,Almon said.
Firms themselves also face political challenges. One early example came in December,when Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said his company wouldn't work with anyone who hires Biden-era officials

"They are more sensitive to this now than they have been in the past," said law firm consultant Peter Zeughauser."They are more careful."

添司添“Firms are peacocking their Trump feathers in a way we haven't seen in recent memory," said Dan Binstock, a legal recruiter based in Washington.

Among the firms eager to champion its D.C. ties is Akin,which has been pitching itself as particularly well placed for the coming administration. Its marketing tools include a new biweekly policy publication on how clients can navigate the postelection landscape.

T top firms were engaged in a poaching war for star partners with expertise that could bring in new portfolios of business. Trump's victory—and the policy upheavals that come with it—only adds to that pressure.

Anthropic  Value Hits  $60 Billion
Continued from page B1
lic stance in favor of robust safety testing for its technology out of concern AI could be misused or unintentionally cause catastrophic harm to humanity.

E Much of Anthropic's funding to date has come from its close partner Amazon.com,which committed $4 billion to the startup in November,bringing its total investment since 2023 to $8 billion. Amazon's November investment
was a convertible note, meaning it will translate to equity at the valuation set in the current round.

Anthropic develops and operates its technology in data centers operated by Amazon and Google, which also has invested billions of dollars in the startup. The startup's annualized revenue—an extrapolation of the next 12 months' revenue based on recent sales—recently hit about $875 million,one of the knowledgeable people said. Most of that has come from sales to businesses.
OpenAI told investors when closing its October round that it expected to generate $3.7billion in revenue last year, in large part from sales of the premium version of ChatGPT to consumers.
GFL Environmental to  Sell Services Business  In $5.6 Billion Deal
pected to close in the first quar ter.
BY DENNY JACOB

GFL Environmental  has reached an agreement to sell its environmental services business in a deal valued at 8 billion Canadian dollars, equivalent to about $5.59 billion. though it would retain an eguity interest

Ta which transports and recycles waste, among other services,said it reached the agreement with funds managed by affiliates of Apollo and BC Partners.It would retain a 44% equity interest in the business, while funds managed by affiliates of Apollo and BC each would hold a 28% equity interest.
司cluding interested directors who have recused themselves,has unanimously approved the transaction. The deal is ex

E GFL expects cash proceeds from the deal to be around C$6.2 billion. It intends to use up to C$3.75 billion of the net proceeds to repay debt, leaving up to C$2.25 billion for share repurchases. The balance will go to transaction fees and gen eral corporate purposes.

Chief Executive Patrick Dovigi said a deleveraged balance sheet will provide GFI with more financial f exibility to deploy capital into organio growthinitiatives and solid waste M&Aandallowfor greater return of capital to shareholders.
Dovigi said the company wil maintain an option to repur chase the environmental ser vices busines within five years of closing.
The latest proposal from Cintas, a maker of workplace products, was initially given to UniFirst's board in November.
Cintas Bids  For Rival  UniFirst
Continued from page B1
engage with UniFirst, including by stating its willingness to consider increasing its offer price, a subsequent letter says.

Ta 
UniFirst has told Cintas that its board unanimously concluded that a deal wasn't in the best interest of the company and its stakeholders,according to correspondence in December and November.
In October, UniFirst had said in a news release that it was excited about the investments it was making and believed its business had "significant long-term potentiall."
For Cintas, a deal would allow the combined companies to serve more customers and make greater use of its
recent investments in technology and infrastructure.
Ea tors would like the deal: 79%of UniFirst common shares are held by investors who also own Cintas's stock, according to a Cintas analysis included in the letters delivered to UniFirst's board.
A deal for UniFirst, should it happen, would be Cintas's biggest transaction yet.
Ta utive Officer Todd Schneider,who has held that role since  June 2021.
